title: The Shift to Agentic UX: Do the Thinking For Me
author: Dirkjan Kraan
text:
The shift to agentic UX: do the thinking for me
This article explores how agents help could help us shift from traditional feature-driven software to contextual UI patterns.
I am a product designer, and from the first release of ChatGPT I have been wondering: Are we going to prompt our way through the internet from now on?
Prompting is powerful — but also exhausting. Users must constantly translate intentions into structured requests and learn how the system works just to benefit from it. This is never how UX was supposed to be, right?
That’s when I started noticing a shift: instead of humans telling the system what to do, what if systems could finally help humans with the thinking and work?
This article explores how agents could help us shift from traditional feature-driven software to contextual Agentic UI patterns.
What agents really are
Agents have been described in many ways, but here’s how I would describe it:
Agents are a piece of software that can act on behalf of a user or system and can take steps toward a goal — not just answer a question¹.
Now, I’m not going into depth here, but you can have different agents. To name a few: agents that act as a copilot and enhance your productivity and capabilities, and very specific agents focused on a particular automation workflow. The main value of agents isn’t that they respond — it’s that they can also initiate.
How agents work
AI agents use different AI models to perform their tasks, but it can also be as simple as using databases, API endpoints, files — you name it. Working with an AI agent generally involves the following steps, based on a model that McKinsey created¹:
- A user or system gives an agent a task
In this step, the agent receives a task. Remember, this doesn’t necessarily need to come from a human — it can also be triggered by another system. But when it is a human requesting the agent to perform a task, the request is given in natural language. In that case, the agent works with LLMs equipped with natural-language-processing capabilities, and therefore understands what users mean. - The agent plans, allocates, and executes tasks.
This could also happen within a multi-agent ecosystem with specialized sub-agents. For example, they can review each other’s output before returning it to the user. - The agent shares the output with the user.
This is the moment a user can give feedback. This step is optional. For example, if it’s content-heavy, you may want to review it. But if it’s a simple recurring task — like reminding you of something every day — there’s nothing to review. - The agent executes action
In the last step, the agent actually executes the action that the system or user requested.
The maturity of agents
Although agents are in their early stage, they are evolving rapidly. When I mentioned that agents can act on behalf of a user, it doesn’t mean this is always what the user needs. The example above shows a process in which the user initiates, but it could also be the agent that initiates.
This is a bit technical, but to give context — and to understand where we are in our ‘agent readiness’ — several maturity models have been created by, for example, Salesforce², UX Tigers³ (Nielsen Norman), and Architecture Governance⁴. I took the liberty of combining these models, which gives you the following model:
This model gives a sense of feeling of the shift we should start to make when designing experiences for users. As agents progress, so should we. Later I’ll show you how this could translate in UI patterns.
It’s not about the agent
Within teams — and that includes me — we sometimes get excited about the technology itself. We say things like: “We need an agent in our product.” It sounds bold, futuristic, and nice, but it reminds me of the times when everyone said: “We need an app!”
It sounds like a broken record, but the real question is: What problem does it solve? Because let’s be honest, no user wants to interact with an agent. No one wakes up hoping to engage with an agentic interface. They wake up needing to get a job done: improve budgets, close a month, check performance, return items, etc.
So instead of thinking about how to insert agents, we should be thinking about how to remove friction. Agents should help us reduce the moments where users stop and think, “But how?” — whether they asked for help or not.
So what does Agentic UI actually look like?
But what if we do want to improve our user experience by using agents? Because technology — in this case AI agents — enables us to build better software. So how would that look?
Of course, it depends entirely on the workflow of your application. Boiling it down, you can recognize four different Agentic UI patterns. And they can all coexist in the same product, depending on user needs. These patterns come from looking at how today’s AI tools actually support users. They’re based on real patterns I see across AI tools like ChatGPT, Copilot, Notion, Google Workspace, and others.
Pattern 1 — Agent-Powered UI
Familiar UI, but way smarter.
The layout doesn’t change — but everything inside it is intelligently updated. Think of prioritized lists, overnight summaries, next best actions, and more. This pattern feels very familiar, yet the features are significantly smarter. Instead of the user drawing conclusions based on what they see, the agent(s) have already done this — and all you need to do is review.
This patterns helps when the goal is clear and actions become more predictable. Agent-Powered UI gives a clear overview and reduces repetition of certain interactions.
Pattern 2 — Conversational UI
“Tell me what you need — I’ll figure out how.”
The UI guides the user with structured questions and clarification, like:
“What’s your goal?” → “What’s the deadline?” → “How should we format it?”
This is similar to how we experience many AI platforms today — highly conversational.
This pattern helps when users may be unsure and need clarity: “What am I even supposed to do here?” → Conversation Interface could shape the intention.
Pattern 3 — Embedded Assistants
Proactive support at the right moment.
This UI behaves the most like a traditional agent — a bit like a copilot or virtual co-worker. It doesn’t appear to chat, but to help, based on context.
For example: “You’re editing a contract — want me to check compliance?”
Or: “Good morning, your campaign performed well, but I recommend changing XYZ.”
This pattern is useful when it comes to complexity — approvals, exceptions, data checks, etc. An embedded assistant steps in when something needs intervention.
Pattern 4 — Hybrid Agentic UI
A combination of the patterns above that is contextual.
If you zoom out and look at your entire workflows — not just a screen or a moment — you’ll notice something: users move through different mental modes as they progress toward a goal. Each moment and need requires a different way of interacting. Of course, there are consistent patterns, and you need to make sure users know what to expect in certain situations, but each problem still needs to be tackled on its own.
Ultimately, I believe we will move toward interfaces in which agents perform tasks and actions on the user’s behalf and only show what truly matters.
Why combine different patterns? Because users don’t operate in a single mode. A great agentic product matches the moment — which is contextual by nature.
A one-day design challenge
Together with a team at a client, we challenged ourselves with the question: “How could our B2B portal work using agentic technology?” Inspired by Shopify’s Sidekick⁵, we decided to focus on a single user need and explore how this could work so we could build a POC.
We quickly noticed that problems could be solved in multiple ways, but the real opportunity was in how agents could act as a guide to remove friction for the user. Once we let go of the idea that agentic UI is only about chatting with an agent — almost without noticing — we started sketching a Hybrid Agentic UI that combined the different patterns.
Below are some examples of the sketches we ended up with.
A final thought & key take aways
In traditional digital experiences, we create software to support users in their journey. But we’ve expected them to do the necessary thinking, planning, and navigating to complete a task — even though we never intended it to be that way. We designed systems that users had to operate.
With AI and agentic UI, we are moving toward a situation where AI agents can take on that thinking and work for users.
Key take aways
- Users don’t want to “use AI” — they want their work… done. What remains critical is that we still need to understand the actual problem we’re solving. Only then can we design an experience that removes friction and let agents do the thinking.
- Agentic UI isn’t the goal — better workflows and outcomes are the goal. There are many ways to design an interface, and we often end up with different patterns because user needs are contextual.
- Know what you want to solve, and the interactions will follow. — As we’ve all learned with prompting — and with building any kind of product — everything starts with the right context and a clear understanding of the problem to solve. That doesn’t change when designing agentic UI.
Did you like this article? Please feel free to comment or clap! Special thanks and shoutout to Maaike Molenkamp & Silke Kuilman
Resources
[1]: What is an AI agent?
https://www.mckinsey.com/featured-insights/mckinsey-explainers/what-is-an-ai-agent
[2]: The Agentic Maturity Model: A 4-Step Roadmap for CIOs to Succeed in the Agentic Era
https://www.salesforce.com/news/stories/agentic-maturity-model/
[3]: Hello AI Agents: Goodbye UI Design, RIP Accessibility
https://www.uxtigers.com/post/ai-agents
[4]: Examining the Five Maturity Levels of Agentic AI
https://www.architectureandgovernance.com/applications-technology/examining-the-five-maturity-levels-of-agentic-ai/
[5]: How Shopify is Leveraging AI at Scale with Vanessa Lee
https://open.spotify.com/episode/2QO2JN9o1VG6m7Zx4vAfkm?si=2deeed6e47344560
